subject: [GLOBAL] Move to Gitlab
commit_message:
[GLOBAL] Move to Gitlab

Add all necessary files in order to move OOM repo to gitlab and also
uses gitlab-ci features.

Issue-ID: OOM-1
Signed-off-by: Sylvain Desbureaux <sylvain.desbureaux@orange.com>
Change-Id: Ie3ba8d22828b103219d590886a8c58825eff2eef

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

(11 comments)

> Patch Set 6:
> 
> (1 comment)
> 
> for shell scripts vs precommit, I propose to wait for first results and if we see some diffs, I'll create a docker with precommit in it and use it instead of the other scripts

Please can you give me more details about that?
I am not sure there would be an added value in a simple comparison directly on this repo.
To have a serious idea of pros and cons of these 2 approaches, the comparison should be based on a different set of data that should include voluntary errors specially crafted (and even ideally several sets of data of this kind.)
I already took care of cleaning every tabs and trailing blanks etc
As a result, both approaches will very likely pass and gives the same result here...

There are also some very rare cases where tabs and trailing blanks should be allowed, for example Makefile and patch files generated from git diff. I don't understand the point of maintaining a list of such exceptions inside shell scripts when dedicated projects have already taken care of that in a portable language and/or have proposed clear configuration files with regex.

Anyway, I can see in this change several very different topics:
- checkbashisms commands migration to a dedicated script
- linters migration/activation in Gitlab-CI
- Helm and Gate move to Gitlab-Ci
- replacement of tox python linters by dedicated shell scripts
  and split in several Gitlab-Ci sections similar to the actual Jenkins configuration

I propose to split that in several changes to ease the review and their merge.
I've already started to work on the first 3 topics and I am proposing some fixes of small issues identified here in them.
Please can you rebase on top of https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126205

Hope this helps

Patch Set 8:

(7 comments)

Hello Guillaume,

I see that we have 3 main disagreements:

* a single "big" patch. Most of it is the add of gitlab files (ci, markdown, ...) so I think it's a real "single entity" and it cannot be cut down as we'll use either gitlab or jenkins. half a gitlab is not possible.

* not use of tox in the CI. As already said, tox is a wrapper that can makes sense when you're using shared systems (such as jenkins shared runners). In other CI systems (travis ci, gitlab ci, ...), the isolation is done at job level and then you don't need another wrapper. 
  It doesn't mean that we remove tox.ini but it means that we won't use it in CI. The consequence is that we need to be sure that tox.ini files is aligned with ci file (gitlab-ci.yml here) and that's what we'll do

* use of 2 custom scripts instead of precommit script for tabs and trailing whitespaces detection. Here I think that creating a Dockerfile just for these 2 functions (38 and 39 lines in bash, license included and 37/102 lines in Python) doesn't worth the efforts. 
  You can argue that the shell scripts supports only super basic whitelist but today there  are no need on trailing whitespaces of such whitelists and the one for tabs is only on Makefile (the other part of your whitelist are not existent in OOM repository)
  Again, precommit will not disappear and anyone wanting to use it (with or without tox) will be able to use it.
  But it won't be used in the CI.
  My plan is to reassess every couple of monthes if we have a deviation between what has been found by the 2 ci scripts and pre commit. If we have such deviation, then we'll move to precommit also in CI.


I do acknowledge that you don't agree with this patch but the OOM committers have decided to move on. Therefore, I'll submit this patch as soon as Krzysztof puts back his +2.

Patch Set 8:

(10 comments)

Hello

my feedback in the text below.

Hope this helps
Guillaume

> Patch Set 8:
> 
> (7 comments)
> 
> Hello Guillaume,
> 
> I see that we have 3 main disagreements:
> 
> * a single "big" patch. Most of it is the add of gitlab files (ci, markdown, ...) so I think it's a real "single entity" and it cannot be cut down as we'll use either gitlab or jenkins. half a gitlab is not possible.
> 

I think that changes 126205 126240 126207 and 126199 have demonstrated the absolute opposite i.e. this change can perfectly be split. I can see apart the Gitlab migration itself at least these actions:
  - checkbashims commands move to a dedicated script
  - clean-up of some obsolete code
  - a pylintrc creation and the enforcement of pylint
  - the split of the linters job in tox into several git section (though this point is discutable since this is also the Jenkins situation)
  - the replacement of some pre-commit repo by shell scripts in CI

If you think that there is good reason why any of this point is somehow linked to the GitLab migration itself, my opinion ist that this should be more developped in the commit message.


> * not use of tox in the CI. As already said, tox is a wrapper that can makes sense when you're using shared systems (such as jenkins shared runners). In other CI systems (travis ci, gitlab ci, ...), the isolation is done at job level and then you don't need another wrapper. 
>   It doesn't mean that we remove tox.ini but it means that we won't use it in CI. The consequence is that we need to be sure that tox.ini files is aligned with ci file (gitlab-ci.yml here) and that's what we'll do

I think the term wrapper is a bit reductive to what tox brings in term of isolation and dependencies management. But honestly, I am less worried here by the absence of tox than by the regression brought by this change in term of (both CI and OS) portability and maintenability, especially configuration  and code or functions duplications .

> 
> * use of 2 custom scripts instead of precommit script for tabs and trailing whitespaces detection. Here I think that creating a Dockerfile just for these 2 functions (38 and 39 lines in bash, license included and 37/102 lines in Python) doesn't worth the efforts.

If so, why not running all pre-commit and tox jobs in the same and only container ?
These would reduce drastically the number of docker images proposed here.
The configuration in  https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126207/1 took only 6 minutes 9 seconds to perform all tox and pre-commit linter actions in Gitlab-ci.

>   You can argue that the shell scripts supports only super basic whitelist but today there  are no need on trailing whitespaces of such whitelists and the one for tabs is only on Makefile (the other part of your whitelist are not existent in OOM repository)

Agreed this is details (but this does not give any guarantee the rest of the whitelist won't be found in the future).

>   Again, precommit will not disappear and anyone wanting to use it (with or without tox) will be able to use it.
>   But it won't be used in the CI.
>   My plan is to reassess every couple of monthes if we have a deviation between what has been found by the 2 ci scripts and pre commit. If we have such deviation, then we'll move to precommit also in CI.

My feeling is that this is not completely coherent.
From a maintanibility and troubleshooting standpoint, if precommit or tox are really so unadapted to the CI, I would prefer they simply disappear from the codebase and that we keep only one solution so that we avoid code/configuration/functions duplications and so that contributors directly use the solution enforced in the CI.

> 
> 
> I do acknowledge that you don't agree with this patch but the OOM committers have decided to move on. Therefore, I'll submit this patch as soon as Krzysztof puts back his +2.

I invite you to read the following link in OpenStack wiki.
https://wiki.openstack.org/wiki/GitCommitMessages
It sums up pretty well some problems I see here and it well explains the importance to segment one's work in several atomic commit/pieces to ease the review and to identify potential flaws or regressions among other things.
As it is, I think this change doesn't meet the criteria of the OpenStack community to be merged at least because it does not describe enough in its commit message the (non-trivial) actions performed and the reason why.

I agree that the commit message is not best-in-class standards of various open source communities. Normally I'd probably ask Sylvain to refine that but provided that I'm available only until 23rd of December and then I'll need to step down for 6 months I don't want to block Sylvain with this transition to gitlab as with only a PTL this process can be super painful for him.
Change has been successfully merged by Sylvain Desbureaux

Normally I'd probably ask Sylvain to refine that but provided that I'm available only until 23rd of December and then I'll need to step down for 6 months I don't want to block Sylvain with this transition to gitlab as with only a PTL this process can be super painful for him.

Thanks Krzysztof
I appreciate your true and sincere feedback.
And I share your concern about the difficulty to maintain alone a big project such as OOM.
Even 2 people is a too few IMO.
I agree dealing with such a migration is quite stressfull and painful in these conditions.
One week is quite fast, especially if a pipeline run last 6 hours...

I don't want to block you with that. But if you really need some support,
I am actually  convinced this change cannot be reviewed by anyone external to the project 
in one big block as it is pushed here. 
As a GitLab newbie I did not manage to make it work in my sandbox out-of-the-box.
Maybe some configuration options in your GitLab instances are missing.
If so, some guidance would have been helpful.

Apart the divergence on the orientation taken with tox,
I already detected a few issues in this review but there are probably more.
So I had no other choice to split this change to evaluate how those new profiles behave in my Gitlab sandbox.
I did actually succeed to make most of them work without going through the whole migration.
So I disagree with the impossibility to deal with such a problematic step by step.

Anyway, as a result, it seems some profiles were not really enforced and some entrypoint had to be overriden to make this configuration work.
I'll push you my whole rework of this change with the topic "tox-removal-gitlab-migration-split".
(I share Cédric's comments and point of view on tox defacto removal. Using tox as a mitigation layer should be encouraged so that external contributors can reproduce the CI behavior locally.)
I propose you have a look at it.
Feel free to comment it and to reuse whatever material it brings at your convenience if you may encounter the same issues.

Hope this helps

I wish you a merry Christmas to you and your family.
Guillaume
